text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 14 September 2025 

Dr Amelia Rhodes
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Amelia Rhodes
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Walker, DOB: 14-03-1989, Hospital No: KCH928374 NHS No. 9475123068  
12 Fernbrook Gardens, London, SE24 8QJ

Thank you for referring this patient for neurological review. I met him today in clinic accompanied by his partner. He described longstanding episodes that he and his family have termed “spells,” beginning in his late teens. The events are stereotyped: he experiences a brief warning of a rising internal tension and a sense of detachment, followed by behavioural arrest with fixed gaze and lip chewing reported by witnesses. On some occasions there is right arm stiffening and a brief cry. He is typically confused for 15–30 minutes afterwards and complains of a throbbing headache with pronounced fatigue.

Regarding periodicity, he and his partner report that the events tend to group together over several days in a repeating pattern roughly every four to five weeks. Within these burst periods the number of episodes varies and has not been reliably logged; outside these periods he may go a week or two without events. This “regular bursts every few weeks; number during bursts not well documented” pattern has been their usual experience for years. He started levetiracetam six months ago after trials of lamotrigine and carbamazepine were discontinued for rash and dizziness respectively. Notably, since commencing current therapy the overall pattern has persisted without clear change in frequency or recovery time.

There is no consistent relationship with sleep deprivation or alcohol, though he suspects that high work stress and missed meals may act as triggers. No tongue laceration or incontinence has been witnessed. He has had two emergency attendances in the last year for cluster days with prolonged confusion; bloods at those times were unremarkable and a CT head performed three years ago was reported normal. He has never had status epilepticus or significant injury from these events. Family history is non-contributory. He does not drive and is aware of DVLA guidance as previously discussed with his GP.

On examination today he was alert and oriented with normal speech, cranial nerves intact, no focal motor deficit, and normal coordination and gait. Cardiovascular and general examination were unremarkable. There were no features of ongoing post-event confusion at the time of review. Blood pressure 126/78 mmHg, pulse 72 bpm regular.

We discussed next steps. The main issue at present is to clarify the nature of the events and obtain objective correlation. He is agreeable to an ambulatory EEG with event diary, followed by consideration of inpatient video-EEG if the ambulatory study does not capture typical episodes. I have also recommended structured documentation using a calendar or smartphone app to record the timing of warning symptoms, event duration, and recovery features, so that we can quantify numbers during the clustered periods. He will continue levetiracetam at his current dose as he reports tolerability but no perceived benefit so far; we will not change treatment until we have more diagnostic information. I have requested routine bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, vitamin D) to be done at his GP surgery for baseline monitoring.

For safety, we reinforced standard advice around adequate sleep, regular meals, moderated alcohol intake, and seeking urgent help if any event lasts more than five minutes, occurs back-to-back without recovery, or if he sustains significant injury. He understands not to swim unsupervised, to avoid heights, and to take showers instead of baths.

I will review him in clinic after the ambulatory EEG. Thank you again for the referral and for your ongoing care. Please let me know if there is any additional history or prior imaging that would be helpful to obtain before telemetry.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: group together over several days in a repeating pattern roughly every four to five weeks. Within these burst periods the number of episodes varies and has not been reliably logged

attributes.Label: 1 cluster per 4 to 5 week, multiple per cluster

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes stereotyped events that occur in clusters: they "group together over several days" and recur "roughly every four to five weeks." It also states that within these bursts the number of episodes is not reliably logged. Therefore, the best normalized label is a cluster frequency of 1 per 4 to 5 weeks with an unknown count during each cluster, which per rules is expressed as "multiple per cluster." No other more frequent rate is provided.
seizure_frequency_number: 1 cluster per 4 to 5 week, multiple per cluster
group together over several days in a repeating pattern roughly every four to five weeks. Within these burst periods the number of episodes varies and has not been reliably logged
reference: 1 cluster per month, multiple per cluster

flaws.description: Gold (1 cluster per 4 to 5 week, multiple per cluster) disagrees with Reference (1 cluster per month, multiple per cluster)
